coredata.dc:title: Investigation of inventive Quinoxaline-based fluorescent probe for detection of harmful chromium (VI) present in water and its bioapplications

coredata.dc:description: Chromium (VI), well known for its risks to human health and ecosystems, requires monitoring. A novel water-compatible fluorescent sensor based on N-Arylcarbamate-Substituted indolo[1,2-a]quinoxalines (QL) has been developed to detect Cr(VI). QL exhibited a favorable quantum yield of 0.26 when excited at (λex) 360 nm, emitting bluish-green fluorescence at the emission maximum (λem) of 498 nm. Time-Correlated Single Photon Counting (TCSPC) analysis has revealed static quenching mechanism between QL and Cr(VI), suggesting the formation of stable complex. QL has demonstrated remarkable selectivity for Cr(VI) over other interfering ions in a 30 % H2O in DMSO (v/v) solvent system. Job’s plot and mass spectra analyses have indicated a binding stoichiometry of 1:1 between QL and Cr(VI). The interaction between QL and Cr(VI) has been reversible upon the addition of ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), with the short and long-term stability of QL, QL-Cr(VI) complex and regenerated QL being confirmed. QL's linear range spanned from 6.79 to 37.39 µM for Cr(VI), with an impressive limit of detection (LOD) of 19 nM (5 ppb). Sensing of Cr(VI) has been successfully demonstrated in various samples, including drinking water, pond water, urine, milk, blood and juice samples. Further, this endeavour has employed two zooplankton models, Dioithona rigida (marine water) and Daphnia pulex (fresh water), to assess Cr(VI) sensing in aquatic ecosystems and bio-imaging. Notably, this study represents an alternative to existing methods by offering superior sensitivity, rapid detection, and compatibility with real sample matrices. This innovative sensor holds promise for environmental monitoring and safety assessments related to Cr(VI) contamination.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Chromium (VI), well known for its risks to human health and ecosystems, requires monitoring. A novel water-compatible fluorescent sensor based on N-Arylcarbamate-Substituted indolo[1,2-a]quinoxalines (QL) has been developed to detect Cr(VI). QL exhibited a favorable quantum yield of 0.26 when excited at (λex) 360 nm, emitting bluish-green fluorescence at the emission maximum (λem) of 498 nm. Time-Correlated Single Photon Counting (TCSPC) analysis has revealed static quenching mechanism between QL and Cr(VI), suggesting the formation of stable complex. QL has demonstrated remarkable selectivity for Cr(VI) over other interfering ions in a 30 % H2O in DMSO (v/v) solvent system. Job’s plot and mass spectra analyses have indicated a binding stoichiometry of 1:1 between QL and Cr(VI). The interaction between QL and Cr(VI) has been reversible upon the addition of ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), with the short and long-term stability of QL, QL-Cr(VI) complex and regenerated QL being confirmed. QL's linear range spanned from 6.79 to 37.39 µM for Cr(VI), with an impressive limit of detection (LOD) of 19 nM (5 ppb). Sensing of Cr(VI) has been successfully demonstrated in various samples, including drinking water, pond water, urine, milk, blood and juice samples. Further, this endeavour has employed two zooplankton models, Dioithona rigida (marine water) and Daphnia pulex (fresh water), to assess Cr(VI) sensing in aquatic ecosystems and bio-imaging. Notably, this study represents an alternative to existing methods by offering superior sensitivity, rapid detection, and compatibility with real sample matrices. This innovative sensor holds promise for environmental monitoring and safety assessments related to Cr(VI) contamination.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Graphical depiction of the sensing mechanism of QL towards Cr(VI) via the "On-Off" pathway, illustrating fluorescence quenching upon interaction, and subsequent reusability following the addition of EDTA via the "Off-On" pathway, showcasing fluorescence recovery.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Chromium is a redox-active element that exists in several oxidation states, among which hexavalent chromium [Cr(VI)] is the most toxicologically significant. Unlike its trivalent counterpart, Cr(VI) exhibits remarkable environmental mobility and cellular uptake, making it a potent toxicant in both environmental and biological systems [1,2]. Classified as a Group I human carcinogen by the IARC, Cr(VI) is known to act as a co-carcinogen through mechanisms involving DNA damage, gene mutation, oxidative stress, and apoptosis induction [3–5]. Upon cellular entry, Cr(VI) undergoes stepwise reduction to Cr(III), generating a burst of reactive oxygen species (ROS) in the process, culminating in widespread oxidative injury to lipids, proteins, and nucleic acids [6,7]. Industrial processes such as electroplating, chromate dye synthesis, wood preservation, leather tanning, and pigment production rely heavily on Cr(VI)-based compounds (e.g., K₂Cr₂O₇, K₂CrO₄, CrO₅). Unfortunately, improper disposal and runoff have made Cr(VI) a persistent environmental pollutant, frequently contaminating water sources, soil, and atmospheric particulates [3,8–10]. Cr(VI) exposure has been directly linked to respiratory illnesses, nephrotoxicity, hepatotoxicity, dermal lesions, and various cancers, particularly of the lung. Its systemic toxicity is further amplified by its interaction with biomolecular targets and its ability to bypass biological detoxification pathways [11,12]. In aquatic ecosystems, Cr(VI) poses a significant ecological threat by accumulating in sediments and biomagnifying through the food chain, thereby affecting aquatic life and raising serious food safety concerns [13,14]. Recognizing these risks, the United States Environmental Protection Agency (USEPA) and the World Health Organisation (WHO) have established maximum allowable limits of 0.1 mg/L and 0.05 mg/L, respectively, for Cr(VI) in drinking water [15]. These restrictions underscore the urgent need for highly selective, ultrasensitive, and field-deployable detection strategies capable of identifying Cr(VI) contamination at trace concentrations across diverse environmental and biological matrices [16].

Conventional analytical techniques such as Inductively Coupled Plasma Mass Spectrometry (ICP-MS), Inductively Coupled Plasma Atomic Emission Spectroscopy (ICP-AES), Cold Vapor Atomic Absorption Spectroscopy (CVAAS), Atomic Absorption Spectroscopy (AAS), Capillary Electrophoresis (CE), X ray Fluorescence Spectroscopy (XFS), Ion Chromatography coupled with UV-Vis or Mass Photometry detection (IC-UV-Vis MP), High Performance Liquid Chromatography (HPLC) when coupled with ICP-MS and electrochemical sensing platforms are well established for their accuracy and sensitivity in quantifying heavy metals. Despite their analytical robustness, these methods often require elaborate sample preparation, significant financial investment, trained operators, and large-scale instrumentation that limit portability and on-site deployment. Many of these techniques require hazardous chemicals, strong acids, or sources of ionizing radiation. They also need controlled laboratory settings and complicated calibration steps. This makes them difficult to use for quick or field analysis and raises safety and environmental concerns [17–19]. To circumvent these inherent drawbacks, fluorescence based sensing approaches have garnered substantial attention as pragmatic alternatives for heavy metal ion detection. Fluorometric methods offer several compelling advantages: they are cost efficient, operationally straightforward, and capable of delivering rapid, highly sensitive and selective responses often in real time and under ambient conditions. These systems usually need little preparation of samples, use methods that do not harm or invade the materials being tested, and can be easily adapted for portable and field use. Their versatility further extends to live cell imaging, environmental diagnostics, and point of care analysis, making them exceptionally valuable in contexts where speed, simplicity, and spatial resolution are of paramount importance [20]. Fluorometric sensors are essential tools in both scientific research and analytical chemistry. They allow researchers to explore the complexities of the molecular world, leading to significant breakthroughs in fields such as biology, environmental science, and medicine [21]. Fluorometry relies on measuring the fluorescence emission from a fluorophore that, when interacting with a target analyte, causes a change in its fluorescence properties. This modification can be exploited to develop chemosensors that exhibit a fluorescent response [22].

Over the years, researchers have developed numerous novel and cost-effective chemosensors for the accurate and selective detection of chromium ion (Cr(VI)). These sensors encompass various types, such as quantum dots [23], rhodamine derivatives [24], metal-organic framework-based sensors [25], [26], molecular logic gates [27], polymer-based sensor [28] and schiff base-based chemosensors [29]. In addition to these, other classes of sensors have also been employed, including carbon [30,31], functionalized nanoparticles [32], nanoclusters [33], Covalent Organic Frameworks (COFs) [34], ionic liquid-based sensors [35], and calixarene-based receptors [36], etc. Many methods for detecting Cr(VI) have been developed recently, but most sensors still face significant challenges that limit their use. One major issue is that they often depend on complicated and time-consuming production processes. This makes it hard to produce them on a large scale, leads to lower yields, and increases costs. Many of these systems incorporate environmentally hazardous components or require the use of toxic heavy metals, thereby raising concerns regarding ecological and biological safety. A critical bottleneck remains their poor compatibility with aqueous environments, as the sensing mechanisms often deteriorate or become ineffective in water content media, an essential criterion for environmental and biological monitoring. Moreover, these sensors frequently exhibit suboptimal photostability, prone to photobleaching and structural degradation under prolonged exposure to excitation sources, thereby limiting their operational lifespan. Some ongoing issues include the inability to reverse the process, reuse the materials, and select specific targets in complex mixtures because of interference from other ions or molecules. Additionally, the purification steps after making these materials can be complicated, and they often lack compatibility with biological systems. This limits their use in live cell imaging, testing in living organisms, or evaluating toxicity. Collectively, these constraints underscore the urgent necessity for a new class of chemosensors that can seamlessly integrate high sensitivity, selectivity, water solubility, reversibility, reusability, structural robustness, environmental safety, and biological compatibility, all within a single molecular platform.

In this context, quinoxaline-based system offers a holistic and unprecedented solution to overcome these entrenched limitations. Quinoxaline derivatives are important for making fluorescent sensors because of their special properties. They have high fluorescence quantum yields, strong connections between their molecular parts, excellent stability, and can be easily modified. This allows them to detect substances accurately in complex mixtures. Additionally, derivatives like indole, quinoxaline, and N-arylcarbamates have various biological activities. These include effects that fight infections, cancer, inflammation, and seizures, among other benefits.

Zooplankton are a diverse group in aquatic ecosystems, serving as key indicators of biotic and abiotic pollutants. As primary consumers, Dioithona rigida (marine) and Daphnia pulex (freshwater) indicate ecosystem health by responding rapidly to changes in water quality [37]. Their selection enables for an assessment of acute toxicity, offering insights into the impacts of contaminants across various exposure durations. Research on zooplankton contributes to the development of environmental management strategies and regulatory policies aimed at preserving ecosystems [38]. Dioithona rigida and Daphnia pulex serve as excellent ecological models, providing a holistic view of environmental impact. Zooplankton are important parts of the food web. They move energy from lower levels to higher levels and show how contaminants affect living organisms in natural water settings. Testing zooplankton for toxicity shows how harmful substances impact these creatures and other species that depend on them, like small fish and invertebrates. Studying these primary consumers helps us understand how pollutants affect the entire food chain, which is more relevant to nature than lab tests that often miss important interactions within ecosystems [39]. Zooplankton are highly sensitivity to environmental stressors, making them excellent subjects for acute toxicity studies. Their use provides reliable and well-documented results that reflect real-world contamination effects, allowing for the generation of ecosystem-level toxicity data. Additionally, studying zooplankton enables a comprehensive evaluation of physiological effects, such as survival, reproduction, and behaviour, as well as environmental monitoring, such as chromium detection. These aspects cannot be fully captured by in vitro models, which are limited to isolated biochemical pathways [37].

Herein, we present a novel heterocyclic chemosensor, butyl (2-(9-bromoindolo[1,2-a]quinoxalin-6-yl)phenyl)carbamate (QL), for the highly sensitive and selective detection of Cr(VI). While aqueous solution of potassium dichromate (K₂Cr₂O₇) has been used as the Cr(VI) source, the term Cr(VI) is used throughout for clarity and alignment with scientific conventions [40–42]. QL exhibits a reversible "Turn-Off/On" fluorescence response with Cr(VI) and EDTA, ensuring reusability. Its excellent selectivity ensures minimal interference from other ions, making it ideal for real-world applications. Additionally, QL's practical utility has been demonstrated in complex sample matrices, including environmental and biological systems. The in vivo detection of Cr(VI) has been validated using two zooplankton models: Dioithona rigida and Daphnia pulex. These models demonstrated a high sensitivity to Cr(VI) exposure. The results highlight that QL is an effective tool for environmental monitoring, toxicological studies, and real-time detection of Cr(VI).

All chemicals and reagents used in this study are of analytical grade, conforming to ACS/ ISO standards, and have been procured from certified suppliers Sigma-Aldrich and Sisco Research Laboratory (India). All materials have been used without further purification. A melting point apparatus determined QL's melting point (Mp). Functional groups (ν̅) within the compound have been identified through infrared spectroscopy (IR) using a KBr pellet and an IR spectrometer. To elucidate the hydrogen environment, proton nuclear magnetic resonance (1H NMR) spectra have been recorded at 300 MHz, employing a Bruker instrument with CDCl3 as the solvent. Information regarding the carbon nuclei has been obtained by acquiring carbon-13 nuclear magnetic resonance (13C{1H}NMR) spectra at 75 MHz, also utilizing CDCl3 as the solvent. Additionally, liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry/mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) has been employed to determine QL and QL-Cr(VI)’s molecular mass precisely. Additionally, absorption and emission measurements have been conducted using specialized instruments: a UV-Vis-NIR spectrophotometer (Jasco V-670; Japan) for absorption and a Hitachi F-7100 fluorescence spectrophotometer for both excitation and emission (5 nm slit). D. rigida specimens have been examined using a Confocal Laser Scanning Microscope (Carl Zeiss LSM 900, Germany), while D. pulex specimens have been studied with an Epifluorescence Microscope (Carl Zeiss Axioscope 5, Germany). These instruments have been utilized to gather valuable data regarding the structural, photophysical properties, spectroscopic and biological behaviour of QL and QL-Cr(VI) under investigation. Furthermore, the quantitative analysis of Cr(VI) in drinking water samples has been performed using an Inductively Coupled Plasma Mass Spectrometer (ICP-MS, Agilent 7700, USA) and correlated with the fluorometric method by the developed probe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Generalized experimental method for the synthesis of indolo[1,2-a]quinoxalines with N-arylcarbamate substitution
ce:section.ce:para: The N-Arylcarbamate-Substituted indolo[1,2-a]quinoxalines (QL) employed in this study have been synthesized following a previously reported method by Pramanik et al. from the same research group as shown in Scheme 1 [43]. Briefly, the reaction involved condensation of 2-(5-bromo-1H-indol-1-yl)aniline (1.0 mmol) with indoline-2,3-dione (1.0 mmol) in a 1:1 molar ratio. Detailed reaction conditions, purification procedures, and characterization data for the synthesized QL are provided in the Supporting Information (SI).

The geometry optimization of QL has been performed using the Gaussian software package. The 6–31 +g(d,p) basis set and B3LYP functional have been used for all the atoms involved in the structure. The positive vibrational frequencies confirmed that the optimized structures for both QL and QL-Cr(VI) adduct correspond to global minima. To calculate the absorption spectra, time-dependent density functional theory (TDDFT) with the B3LYP functional has been implemented. The lowest 50 singlet states have been computed for the vertical excitation energy calculations for both QL and QL–Cr(VI) adduct. The 6–31 +g(d,p) basis set is widely recognized for its reliability in accurately modelling the structural, electronic, and optical properties of small organic molecules and their complexes. B3LYP, a hybrid functional, is known to effectively reproduce experimental absorption features with minimal error, making it particularly suitable for studying such systems. Solvent effects have been not included in the present study, as the primary goal has been to assess the general electronic trends, which are known to show minimal deviation between gas and solvent phases for similar systems. Therefore, gas-phase calculations have been performed to ensure computational efficiency without compromising the validity of the results, as supported by literature precedents [44,45].

The experimental methodologies, including stock solution preparation, spectroscopic measurements, quantum yield, binding constant, limit of detection calculations, toxicity assessment, and fluorescence bio-imaging for Cr(VI) detection, are detailed in SI.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties study by experimental analysis
ce:section.ce:para:
The electronic absorption spectrum of QL exhibits major absorption maxima at 280, 360 and 400 nm, with the 280 nm band attributed to intraligand π → π* transition. This involves electron excitation within QL from π bonding to π* antibonding orbital, likely involving its π-electron system. The 360 and 400 nm bands arise from electronic transitions involving non-bonding orbitals on nitrogen and oxygen to π* antibonding orbitals in QL. Lone-pair electrons on heteroatoms interact with nearby π* orbitals, resulting in transitions at longer wavelengths compared to intraligand transitions (Fig. S-3 (a)). QL solution emits bluish-green fluorescence when exposed to UV light, corresponding to the visible spectrum. When excited from 280 to 440 nm (
Fig. 1 (a)), QL shows stable bluish-green emission at 498 nm, indicating a consistent emission wavelength (Fig. S-3 (b)). The consistent emission center confirms that QL emits a specific color (bluish-green), regardless of the excitation wavelength. The stable 498 nm emission band reflects a consistent energy gap between excited and ground states, ensuring a steady emission color. Although excited at various wavelengths (280–440 nm), QL absorbed most efficiently at 360 nm, leading to a higher excited-state population and fluorescence intensity ( Fig. 1 (b)).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties study by density functional theory (DFT)
ce:section.ce:para: The calculated UV-Vis absorption spectra of QL and QL-Cr(VI) adduct are shown in Fig. 1 (d). QL exhibits five absorption peaks at 414, 386, 349, 313, and 279 nm, closely matching experimental values at 424, 384, 358, 340, and 280 nm ( Fig. 1 (c)). Absorption energies and oscillator strengths are listed in Table 1. The orbitals involved are primarily located in the aminoester moiety. Moreover, QL-Cr(VI) adduct shows five absorption peaks at 423, 416, 366, 344, and 281 nm, aligning with experimental results at 428, 396, 374, 346, and 280 nm ( Fig. 1 (c)). The transitions originate primarily from the chromic acid part. Only the LUMO and LUMO+ 1 orbitals show electron density on the chromic acid moiety. Details of the orbital diagrams are included in SI.

To enhance the real-world applicability of QL, a systematic study has been conducted using different H₂O:DMSO fractions, considering that environmental pollutants, including heavy metal ions, are predominantly found in aqueous systems. The investigation of solvent ratios revealed that the emission intensity has been maximized at H₂O:DMSO (3:7) (
Fig. 2 (a)). This enhancement is likely due to improved solvation of QL and a favorable molecular conformation that promotes radiative decay pathways, while minimizing non-radiative losses. Beyond 30 % water, the fluorescence intensity decreased significantly, which may be attributed to aggregation-induced quenching or reduced solubility of QL in high water content [46]. The inclusion of 30 % water ensures QL's compatibility with aqueous environments and enhances its capability to detect Cr(VI) present in water, making the system relevant for practical applications. Despite concerns regarding solvent toxicity, DMSO is widely recognized as a low-toxicity solvent compared to many other commonly used organic solvents, such as dimethylformamide (DMF), acetonitrile (ACN), tetrahydrofuran (THF), methanol (CH3OH), 1,4-Dioxane, N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP), and dichloromethane (DCM), all of which exhibit significantly higher toxicity and pose greater environmental concerns. DMSO is biocompatible and is often used in biological and environmental research. It has excellent solvating properties that facilitate sensor performance. Unlike highly toxic organic solvents, DMSO has low volatility and is non-carcinogenic. It also acts as a drug delivery enhancer and is rapidly metabolized in biological systems, which helps to minimize environmental and health risks [47–49]. Furthermore, the solvent system maintained a neutral pH of 7, without requiring additional buffers. This ensured its suitability for spectroscopic studies by preventing unwanted chemical interactions. Given these advantages, the H₂O:DMSO (3:7) ratio has been chosen as the optimal condition for all spectroscopic studies. This choice enables the effective detection of Cr(VI) in aqueous environments while ensuring both analytical precision and practical feasibility.

The sensor molecule has been prepared in a solution of H2O and DMSO at a volume ratio of 3:7, with pH values ranging from 1 to 12. The fluorescence emission spectra have been recorded at each pH level. We conducted measurements and analyses of the emission intensity and wavelength as they varied with pH. The maximum intensity has been observed at pH 7, and QL's emission intensity has been clearly pH-dependent. The intensity decreased as the pH deviated from this ideal level at both low and high pH conditions. The carbamates group in QL are pH-sensitive due to the protonation and deprotonation of the secondary amine group (-NH-) in response to pH changes. This behavior arises from the acidic nature of the protonatable amine. At low pH, the amine can be protonated (=NH₂⁺), while at high pH, it undergoes deprotonation [50]. The fluorescence properties are optimized in an environment that is close to neutral pH. This is evidenced by the maximum emission intensity observed at pH 7 in Fig. 2 (b). At this pH level, the protonation and deprotonation of QL's carbamate are balanced, which enhances the fluorescence emission.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity and selectivity of QL towards Cr(VI)
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence sensing ability of QL has been investigated by varying the concentration of Cr(VI) ions from 0 to 3 equivalents in a solution of H2O/DMSO (v/v = 3:7, 50 µM, pH 7). As the concentration of Cr(VI) ions increased, there has been a gradual decrease in emission intensity, as shown in Fig. 3 (a). The emission intensity at the wavelength of maximum absorption (λmax = 498 nm) exhibited a strong linear correlation with the concentration of Cr(VI) ions, which ranged from 6.79 µM to 37.39 µM ( Fig. 3 (b)). The correlation coefficient has been R² = 0.998, and the limit of detection (LOD) has been found to be 19 nM, as calculated using equation (S3) referenced in SI. This LOD is well below the maximum permissible limits set by WHO and USEPA standards. QL sensor exhibits remarkable sensitivity in detecting Cr(VI), achieving detection limits at very low nanomolar levels. This significant improvement in sensitivity is apparent when compared to the limits of detection reported by other sensors listed in Table S-6.

Interference experiments have been conducted to evaluate the effect of interfering substances on Cr(VI) detection. In these experiments, inorganic salts containing cations such as Mn(II), Ni(II), Hg(II), Co(II), Zn(II), Cd(II), Al(III), Pb(II), Fe(II), Cu(II), Fe(III), Cr(III), K(I), Mg(II) and Ag(I) have been tested. Additionally, anions including Br-, NO3 -, NO2 -, SO4 2-, PO4 3-, CO3 2-, ACO-, SO3 2-, AsO4 3-, ClO4 2-, S2- and MnO4 - (at 3 equivalents) have been added into QL solution. Despite these additions, the emission intensity remained largely unchanged. QL exhibited exceptional selectivity for Cr(VI), completely quenching fluorescence with the addition of 3 equivalents of Cr(VI), while other ions showed no significant effect (
Fig. 4 , S-4).

Furthermore, fluorescence quenching persisted even in the presence of interfering ions within complex sample matrices ( Fig. 4 (a) and (b)), confirming QL’s capability to selectively detect Cr(VI) in real-world applications. The fluorescence of the [QL-Cr(VI)] complex at 498 nm is clearly distinct, allowing for the identification of Cr(VI) even in the presence of other ions. This high selectivity makes QL a promising sensor for Cr(VI) in complex matrices. The optimal response of QL in detecting Cr(VI) is attributed to its chemical structure, which is designed to interact exclusively with Cr(VI) while excluding other ions, thereby enhancing accuracy and reliability. When Cr(VI) has been introduced, the fluorescence at λmax = 498 nm has been quenched, and the emission color changed from “on” to “off,” resulting in a non-fluorescent state. Such color changes indicate a specific QL-Cr(VI) interaction, confirming the presence of Cr(VI) ( Fig. 4 (c) and (d)).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Plausible sensing mechanism of QL towards Cr(VI)
ce:section.ce:para: The detection of Cr(VI) using QL involves a specific interaction mechanism that results in fluorescence quenching. This sensing mechanism, particularly for Cr(VI) in its chromic acid form (H₂CrO₄), is governed by non-covalent interactions, such as hydrogen bonding, between chromic acid and the functional groups of QL.

When dichromate dissolves in water, it converts into chromic acid [51,52]:
Cr2O7 2- + H2O → 2H2CrO4

In aqueous solutions, Cr(VI) predominantly exist as H₂CrO₄, which are highly electrophilic and exhibit a strong affinity for electron-rich moieties. The interaction between QL and Cr(VI) leads to a stable QL-Cr(VI) complex.

The primary mechanism involves the formation of a stable ground-state complex via hydrogen bonding. In this process, the pyridinic nitrogen in QL acts as a hydrogen bond acceptor, while the hydroxyl groups in H₂CrO₄ serve as donors. Specifically, the pyridinic nitrogen interacts non-covalently with the -OH group of H₂CrO₄, while the secondary amino (-NH) group in QL bonds with the =O groups of H₂CrO₄, resulting in the formation a non-covalent QL-Cr(VI) complex.

Cr(VI)’s high affinity for QL’s electron-dense regions, particularly the pyridine nitrogen and aminoester group, stabilizes QL-Cr(VI) complex. Upon binding, QL undergoes conformational changes, rotating functional groups around the pyridinic nitrogen and aminoester groups to stabilize interactions with Cr(VI). These conformational adjustments are crucial for effective binding.

QL adopts a conformation that reduces fluorescence intensity upon complexation. This non-fluorescent ground-state complex is both energetically favorable and stable. A static quenching mechanism is crucial in this process, where the excited-state energy of QL transfers to Cr(VI) and is dissipated as heat instead of being emitted as fluorescence, following a non-radiative pathway. This phenomenon results in the suppression of fluorescence, which has been confirmed through Time-Correlated Single Photon Counting (TCSPC) analysis.

The formation of the ground-state complex does not significantly alter QL’s electronic structure. Instead, it involves non-covalent interactions and conformational rearrangements that stabilizing the complex. This stability leads to a persistent fluorescence suppression through a process known as static quenching. The reduction in fluorescence intensity via a non-radiative pathway is characteristic of static quenching. In this process, the quencher (Cr(VI)) forms a stable complex with the fluorophore (QL) in its ground state. This interaction decreases the number of fluorescent species available for excitation and emission. Since this quenching takes place prior to excitation, it is appropriately referred to as "static" [53,54].

The analysis of the Job's plot aimed to determine the binding stoichiometry of QL-Cr(VI) complex using varying molar ratios. The highest response has been observed at a 0.5 molar fraction of Cr(VI), indicating that one QL molecule interacts with one Cr(VI) ion for optimal binding. This suggests the formation of a 1:1 complex (
Fig. 5 (a)). In addition, the Benesi-Hildebrand plot determined the association constant (Ka) as 5.97 × 103 M−1 using equation (S2) represented in (SI), confirming QL’s high affinity for Cr(VI) ( Fig. 5 (b)). To gain a deeper understanding of the binding interactions within QL-Cr(VI) complex, mass spectrometric analysis has been conducted. This technique is valuable for elucidating the molecular composition of complexes and confirming their stoichiometry. The analysis revealed that, along with corresponding QL peak at m/z = 498, a new peak at m/z = 606 appeared which corresponds to the ion [QL-H₂CrO₄]⁺ ensembles ( Fig. 5 (c)). This confirmed QL’s complexation with chromic acid (H₂CrO₄), the dominant Cr(VI) form, supporting the proposed 1:1 binding stoichiometry.

The reversibility tests conducted on the sensor provide valuable insights into its reusability. In this study, EDTA has been introduced into the solution containing QL and Cr(VI). After the addition of EDTA, the emission intensity of QL-Cr(VI) complex increased. This suggests free QL regeneration, as shown in Fig. 6 (a) and S-8. EDTA likely chelated Cr(VI) in QL-Cr(VI) complex, releasing QL and restoring fluorescence properties. Furthermore, when Cr(VI) has been reintroduced, the fluorescence quenching returned to the original QL-Cr(VI) intensity. This finding confirms QL as a reversible, reusable sensor for Cr(VI) with suitable complexing agents like EDTA [55], as shown in Scheme 2. To establish the optimal concentration of EDTA required for maximum fluorescence restoration, a detailed titration experiment has been performed by incrementally adding EDTA (5–150 µM) to QL-Cr(VI) system. The fluorescence intensity gradually increased with EDTA concentration and reached saturation at 150 µM, beyond which no significant change has been observed. This indicates that 150 µM is sufficient for complete chelation of Cr(VI) and full regeneration of QL fluorescence, as illustrated in Fig. S-8 (a).

Additionally, a comprehensive stability evaluation has been undertaken to assess the photophysical robustness and sensing reliability of QL across both immediate and extended timeframes. To begin with, a short-term fluorescence stability test has been performed for QL, QL-Cr(VI), and QL-Cr(VI)-EDTA systems over a duration of 30 minutes, with measurements recorded at 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 15, 20, 25, and 30 minutes. As depicted in Fig. 6 (b), the fluorescence intensity remained entirely stable throughout the tested period, without any perceptible decrease, underscoring the excellent short-term operational stability of the system under ambient conditions. In parallel, a long-term stability investigation has been conducted to monitor the durability and reproducibility of the sensor response over an extended period of 30 days, with fluorescence intensities measured at 5-day intervals. The solutions have been refrigerated to preserve structural and photophysical integrity. As shown in Fig. 6 (c), the emission intensity of QL in the presence of Cr(VI) remained consistently quenched throughout the study, validating the persistence of QL-Cr(VI) interaction. The free QL also retained its original emission characteristics, confirming its inherent photostability. Furthermore, the regeneration efficiency of QL upon EDTA treatment remained intact, exhibiting negligible fluorescence loss across the entire duration. Together, these short and long-term studies unequivocally demonstrate the superior stability, reliability, and reusability of QL, establishing its strong potential for practical, real-world applications.

Fluorescence lifetime studies have been examined to analyze the quenching phenomenon of QL towards Cr(VI). The fluorescence decay patterns of QL, both in the presence and absence of Cr(VI), have been examined using time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC). Both QL and QL-Cr(VI) complexes showed biexponential decay behavior. To assess the goodness of fit for the fluorescence decay curves, chi-square values have been calculated and are presented in Table 2 . The analysis revealed that the biexponential decay model adequately described the fluorescence decay behavior of both QL and QL-Cr(VI) complexes, as evidenced by the chi-square values. Upon examining the fluorescence lifetime data, it has been observed that QL exhibited an average fluorescence lifetime of 8.57 ns. This average lifetime value has been attributed to two distinct components with lifetime values of 24.26 ns and 11.73 ns, respectively. After the addition of Cr(VI), the average lifetime slightly decreased to 8.36 ns, indicating changes in the fluorescence decay kinetics. Specifically, Cr(VI) altered the contributions of the two components, resulting in new lifetimes of 16.76 ns and 12.06 ns. Nevertheless, the overall fluorescence lifetime remained nearly unchanged, indicating a static quenching mechanism ( Fig. 6 (d)). The static quenching in QL-Cr(VI) system indicates the formation of a non-fluorescent ground-state complex, as supported by existing literature [56,57].

The ligand structure has been optimized using the 6–31 +g(d,p) basis set and B3LYP functional in DFT. All calculations in this study used the same basis set and functional. The optimized geometries of QL and QL-Cr(VI) adducts are shown in Fig. 7. The metal coordination mode aligns well with both the optimized ligand geometry and experimental data.

The electron density in QL is predominantly found in the conjugated region for both the Highest Occupied Molecular Orbital (HOMO) and Lowest Unoccupied Molecular Orbital (LUMO). The aminoester side chain has minimal influence on the HOMO-LUMO gap. The HOMO-LUMO gap is 3.45 eV for QL and 2.61 eV for QL-Cr(VI) adduct. In the adduct, the HOMO is located on the bromopyridyl moiety, while the LUMO is associated with QL-Cr(VI) residue.

The main interaction between QL and chromic acid occurs via hydrogen bonding. In particular, the basic pyridinic nitrogen of QL acts as a hydrogen bond acceptor, while the O-H bond in chromic acid serves as a hydrogen bond donor.

The dihedral angle between the bromopyridyl fluorophore (C13-C3) and the phenyl group that carries the aminoester side chain (C20-C19) is measured at 52.6° in QL. This angle increases to 64.2° in QL-Cr(VI) adduct, indicating steric relaxation. Additionally, the dihedral angle between pyridinic C-N bond (C19-N2) and aminoester C-N bond (C17-N3) shifts significantly from 32.8° to 92.4° in the 1:1 adduct. This large change suggests that the chromophore rotates to accommodate the chromate ions and enhance the interactions with QL. Furthermore, the bond distance of the aminoester N-H bond (N3-H13) is 1.016 Å, which remains almost unchanged at 1.014 Å in the 1:1 adduct. The N-H hydrogen is shared with chromate oxygen (O3) via a (O•••H) hydrogen bond distance of 2.12 Å and an N-H•••O bond angle of 143.7°. For further clarity, the fully optimized 3D geometries of QL and its Cr(VI) adduct along with their corresponding calculated dihedral angles have been provided in SI (Section 10.3, Fig. S-12 and Table S-3 & 4). These structural parameters reinforce the observed conformational changes upon Cr(VI) coordination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Toxicity and lethal concentration on zoo plankton
ce:section.ce:para: D. rigida and D. pulex are widely used in ecotoxicology due to their high sensitivity to environmental stressors, rapid reproductive rates, and key roles in aquatic ecosystems. As primary consumers in both marine (D. rigida) and fresh water (D. pulex) food webs, they act as essential bio-indicators of ecosystem health, responding quickly to changes in water quality [58,59]. The importance of using these species stems from their capacity to reflect the broader impacts of contaminants on aquatic communities. Their short life cycles enable for quick assessments of acute toxicity, making them effective tools for studying the impacts of pollutants over different exposure durations [60,61]. Additionally, the response of both marine and freshwater organisms to chemical stressors offer valuable insights into potential risks for other aquatic life, including fish and amphibians, supporting the development of environmental management strategies and regulatory policies [62].

In the current study with D. rigida, 25 adult organisms have been exposed to five different concentrations of QL over time intervals of 24, 48, 72, and 96 h. The mortality rate increased with prolonged exposure and higher concentrations.

In a parallel experiment, another set of 25 adult D. pulex has been subjected to the same five concentrations of QL over the same four time periods (24, 48, 72, and 96 h). Mortality rates have been compared to a control group, which had a 100 % survival rate, as shown in Fig. 8 (c). In the treated samples, mortality rates increased with both the duration of exposure and the concentration of QL. The lowest survival rate (fewer than 10 living organisms) has been observed at 96 h with the highest concentration of 100 µg/L, indicating that QL’s toxicity is dependent on both concentration and exposure time. The acute toxicity analysis determined the LC50 (lethal concentration for 50 % of the population) to be 78.7 µg/mL, as shown in Fig. 8 (d).

In contrast to D. pulex, which exhibited a higher sensitivity to QL, 50 % of the D. rigida organisms survived even at the highest concentration of 100 µg/L, as shown in Fig. 8 (a). The acute toxicity analysis revealed that the LC25 (the lethal concentration for 25 % of the population) has been calculated to be 52.64 µg/mL ( Fig. 8 (b)). This indicates that D. rigida has a lower sensitivity to QL compared to D. pulex.

The antioxidant capacity of QL has been assessed against standard ascorbic acid. The DPPH assay has been conducted with QL in triplicates, and a color change from purple to pale yellow has been observed in the compound samples. QL exhibited significant scavenging activity compared to the standard control. Additionally, the percentage of DPPH scavenging activity increased with the rising concentration of QL. At a concentration of 500 μg/mL, the scavenging activity of QL has been determined to be 44 ± 3.3 %. These results confirm the antioxidant activity of QL (Fig. S-17).

In Fig. 9 (a), a series of bright field images captures the dynamic response of the marine zooplankton species D. rigida to a novel compound known as QL. Fig. 9 (b) shows the organism under excitation within the wavelength range of 350 nm to 400 nm (violet light), without exposure to QL, providing a baseline reference. Upon exposure to QL (50 µg/mL which is less than LC50)), as shown in Fig. 9 (c), an immediate transformation in the organism is observed. The subsequent image in Fig. 9 (d), taken 15 minutes after exposure, documents the continued effects of QL on D. rigida.

Turning attention to Fig. 9 (e-f) QL's potential for Cr(VI) sensing is investigated. In Fig. 9 (e) shows the organism imaged immediately after exposure to QL, revealing its initial response. Subsequently, Fig. 9 (f) depicts the introduction of Cr(VI) (50 µg/mL) into the growth solution and the imaging of D. rigida in the presence of both QL and Cr(VI). After a 15-minute incubation period, as illustrated in Fig. 9 (g), the imaging captures the altered state of the organism, shedding light on QL's role in Cr(VI) sensing. These figures collectively provide valuable insights into the real-time impact and efficacy of QL on D. rigida, both in its native state and as a potential tool for environmental sensing, particularly in the context of Cr(VI) detection.

Fig. 10 illustrates the real-time interaction of the freshwater zooplankton D. pulex with the novel compound QL and its subsequent response to Cr(VI). In Fig. 10 (a), a bright-field image captures the native state of D. pulex, while Fig. 10 (b) shows the organism under violet excitation (350–400 nm) prior to any treatment, serving as a baseline. Upon QL exposure (50 µg/mL, which is less than LC25), Fig. 10 (c) reveals immediate physiological changes, which continue to evolve over the next 15 minutes, as observed in Fig. 10 (d).

Further sensing ability of QL is presented in Fig. 10 (e-g). Fig. 10 (e) depicts D. pulex following initial treatment with QL, highlighting its intrinsic fluorescence response. The subsequent addition of Cr(VI) (50 µg/mL) to the system, shown in Fig. 10 (f), initiates a noticeable interaction, which intensifies after 15 minutes of incubation ( Fig. 10 (g)). These progressive changes underscore sensitivity of QL and effectiveness as a Cr(VI) sensor, demonstrating its potential utility in environmental monitoring using D. pulex as a biological model.

The in-vivo testing of Cr(VI) detection has been conducted using both marine and freshwater zooplankton models, demonstrating complete quenching of fluorescence in response to Cr(VI), further emphasizing the enhanced specificity QL for Cr(VI). The study also revealed significant scavenging activity of QL compared to the standard control, underscoring its biological function as an effective antioxidant. Moreover, the reversibility and reusability of QL highlight its practical applicability for repeated use. These findings suggest that QL holds great potential as a reliable and efficient tool for detecting Cr(VI) in a wide range of environmental and biological samples across both marine and freshwater ecosystems.

Six different types of samples including (a) drinking water, (b) pond water, (c) urine sample, (d) milk sample, (e) blood sample and (f) juice sample have been obtained for actual sample analysis. A Cr(VI) solution has been then added into the actual samples to prepare a stock solution. The stock solution has been then diluted by the same actual samples to obtain sufficient concentration for fluorescence analysis. Before performing experiments on real samples, calibration plots or standard curves have been taken using optimized fluorescence titration conditions. The correlation between fluorescence intensity and Cr(VI) concentration has been demonstrated using these calibration plots as reference curves. The excellent accuracy of this fluorescence sensor is demonstrated by the good recovery rate observed in the experiment. The recovery values, ranging from 97.4 % to 103.0 %, along with the observed low RSDs, unequivocally attest to the high accuracy, precision, and robustness of our sensing methodology for Cr(VI) detection in diverse real sample matrices, as detailed in Table 3 . The corresponding linear curves can be viewed in Fig. S-18 (a), (b), (c), (d), (e) and (f). This accuracy strengthens its potential application in environmental water analysis. The ability of the sensor to recover a significant percentage of Cr(VI) indicates its reliability and usefulness for environmental monitoring and assessment. This shows that QL can accurately and successfully identify Cr(VI) ions in various water, biological and other environmental samples, making it a useful tool for environmental research and quality monitoring.

Furthermore, to substantiate the practical reliability of QL sensor, a comparative validation study has been conducted against the standard inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) technique for the detection of Cr(VI) in drinking water samples. The Cr(VI) concentrations have been quantified via the fluorescence approach demonstrated exceptional concordance with those determined by ICP-MS, as presented in Table S-7. Both analytical methods have exhibited highly consistent recovery values within the acceptable range. This close alignment between the fluorescence-based method and the sophisticated ICP-MS not only substantiates the analytical reliability, precision, and robustness of sensing strategy but also accentuates its promise as a viable, cost-effective, and portable alternative to conventional high-end instrumentation for routine surveillance and monitoring of Cr(VI) contamination.

This endeavour presents a significant advancement in the field of environmental sensing through the development of a novel water-compatible fluorescent sensor based on N-Arylcarbamate-Substituted indolo[1,2-a]quinoxalines (QL) for the detection of Cr(VI) present in water at trace concentrations with a remarkable 19 nM (5 ppb) detection limit, surpassing WHO and USEPA standards. QL sensor underscores its cost-effectiveness compared to conventional methods such as AAS, ICP-MS, and XFS etc. Fluorescence quenching in zooplankton confirms that QL binds to Cr(VI) in a manner that depends on both time and concentration. This finding highlights its potential applications in environmental toxicity studies. By using D. rigida and D. pulex as biological models, our research highlights QL’s efficacy in environmental monitoring and ecological assessments. This amalgamation of chemical sensing with biological evaluation offers valuable insights into the interactions between contaminants and aquatic organisms, paving the way for comprehensive environmental risk assessments. The simplicity and efficiency of QL’s synthesis route, coupled with its high sensitivity and selectivity, make it a promising tool for the real-time detection of environmental pollutants. It can effectively detect Cr(VI) in various water samples as well as in real-world environmental samples.
